title: Like Endless Bubbles
description: A quiet, philosophical moment between Hino and Nagafuji after school. Nagafuji shares a dream about time, distance, and how two people can never truly share the same 'now'—then wraps Hino in a hug that makes all the distance disappear.
content:
Oh? Hino got home before me.

Welcome back.

I was lying around in Nagafuji's house reading manga when she walked in—and then instantly leaned back in shock when she saw me. She was still in her school uniform, which was surprising. We practically left school at the same time.

Did you wander around somewhere on the way home?

Hmph. Secret training.

…Training for what.

I asked, then noticed the boomerang in her hand. I decided it was safer to just pretend I didn't see it. Don't tell me she brought that thing to school.

The times may change, but this girl's weirdness never does.

Oh, right.

She took off her glasses before she even removed her shoes, like she suddenly remembered something.

Want to hear something I learned in a dream yesterday?

…You're speaking even more nonsense than usual. By about thirty percent.

I closed my manga, sat up, and watched Nagafuji absentmindedly push up her glasses— except she'd already taken them off.

I'm actually someone from the past.

Is that so? Make sure to look both ways before you time-travel back.

Don't worry, don't worry.

Nagafuji stepped inside without taking off her shoes. Her dad yelled at her to use the back door, but of course she ignored him completely. Then she dropped down on all fours and crawled toward me.

When I was sleeping, someone told me something.

…Who?

No idea, she said, tilting her head. If I chased that question any further, the conversation would never progress.

Fine. Just say it.

My, my. Didn't know you were that eager to hear it. Very good, very good.

Nagafuji tossed her bag aside and fiddled with the boomerang as she continued: The sunlight we see was actually emitted by the sun about eight minutes ago.

Huh? Oh—yeah, I think I've heard that before.

Maybe on TV or in class somewhere. She grinned proudly and went on: As for moonlight, it's a little over one second old.

Oh.

Mhm.

…And?

Is she just flexing random trivia? If it were anyone else I'd expect more, but with Nagafuji, the conversation could very well end there. Unexpectedly, it didn't.

Meaning anything separated by distance—no matter how small—is coming to us from the past. Me, you. Even if you write a ridiculous number of zeros after the decimal point, the distance between two people can never truly be zero.

Oh.

Strangely profound. And probably true. No matter how tiny—no matter how impossible it is for a human to perceive—there's always some sliver of separation between people. No two beings can ever share the exact same now.

That gap includes even oneself.

Maybe that's the real reason humans feel lonely.

So, Hino, how do you feel about Nagafuji, the girl separated from you by countless zeros?

She's a smug cow with big boobs.

Smack. I grabbed; she slapped. Yeah, I had that coming.

That's what I heard in my dream.

Uh-huh…

And then I thought this:

Nagafuji crawled toward me like some predator closing in on prey. Then she wrapped her arms around me and hugged me tight.

These ambush hugs of hers aren't rare, but I still haven't fully gotten used to them.

…What's this about.

Hino's 'now' is getting infinitely close to mine.

Her arms tightened around my back, and she sounded genuinely happy.

I tried to think of something clever to say, but the more words I searched for, the further they drifted from what I felt.

So I settled for something short. Boring. Unimpressive.

…Maybe so.

Her voice, her scent—everything felt like it was coming from inside my own body. It was as if every difference between us had been smoothed away, even the tips of our fingers aligned.

How many zeros did that close up?

A bunch. Too many to count.

Not that she'd remember the number anyway.

Fair enough.

Her head swayed against my shoulder. I didn't have to look to know she was smiling.

Honestly, before she even finished talking—before I responded at all—I could already picture the exact smile she'd make.

Everything we've built up every day… it makes distance feel meaningless, like we've slipped out of the limits of our bodies entirely.

Right now, Nagafuji might just be my zero.

Thanks, whoever-you-are people.

And who were those people supposed to be again…

Asking was pointless, but I mumbled it anyway, closing my eyes. Nagafuji is always closer to me than the moon ever is. Realizing that made me think: Ah. This is nice.

And this quiet, ordinary truth filled me with a deep, simple contentment.
